title: Realizing tunable non-Hermitian skin effects in dynamical quantum systems via the relative phase between multiple time-periodic driving
abstract: We demonstrate how the relative phase between the multiple time periodic driving can decide the emergence and the favorable localization direction of non-Hermitian skin modes. For the static non-Hermitian quantum chain with parity time symmetry, non-Hermitian skin effects (NHSEs) can be prohibited. As the dynamical driving is turned on, NHSEs get artificially reactivated, where the relative phase can serve as the controlling switch by breaking the temporal symmetry constraints. Meanwhile, a change of relative phase can also alter the spatial structures of the long-time averaged effective Hamiltonian, which will consequently lead to the variation of skin localization direction for systems of higher dimensions. Our formalisms can be generally realized in diverse optical and mechanical platforms, and will pave the way for realizing tunable skin density profiles.
summary: This paper shows that the relative phase between different periodic drives can be used to turn the non-Hermitian skin effect on or off. It also demonstrates that this phase can control the direction in which particles accumulate at the boundaries in 2D systems. This discovery offers a new way to engineer skin density profiles in platforms like optical lattices or circuit lattices.
main_problem: Controlling the emergence and the localization direction of the non-Hermitian skin effect (NHSE) in dynamical quantum systems.
main_result: The relative phase between multiple time-periodic driving components can act as a switch to activate or deactivate NHSE and can alter the spatial direction of skin mode localization in higher dimensions.
method: Floquet theory and Magnus expansion are used to analyze the long-time averaged effective Hamiltonian and the winding number topological invariant.
model_or_system: Non-Hermitian quantum chains and 2D lattices (such as the NH-QWZ model) subject to multiple time-periodic driving (Floquet systems) with non-reciprocal tunneling.
key_observables: Winding number (C), Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), and skin density profile.
important_parameters: Relative phase (phi) between driving components, driving frequency (omega), and tunneling amplitudes.
main_assumptions: High-frequency limit (omega >> Hamiltonian scales) for the Magnus expansion; focus on PT-symmetric systems.
figures_summary: Figure 1 demonstrates the switching of NHSE on and off by tuning the relative phase and shows the corresponding change in the winding number.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of NHSE control, presents a 1D model demonstrating phase-induced switching, extends the analysis to 2D systems for directional control, and discusses potential experimental realizations.
why_interesting: It provides a novel mechanism for Floquet engineering of non-Hermitian topology, offering a way to dynamically tune the spatial distribution of modes in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
